  "weights": {

  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To make this stronger, should the focus be (A) wording-only polish for clarity and punch without changing structure or claims, (B) reframing the contribution/novelty and tightening the argument (may rephrase strength of claims), or (C) sharpening evidence–claim alignment and quantitative emphasis (calibrating claims to the reported deltas and parameter counts)? You can choose one or multiple (A/B/C).",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening likely involves sharpening the thesis and contribution framing so the core innovation and its motivation are unmistakable.",
    "R1.2": "Ensuring a tight argument from the parameter-sharing idea to expected benefits and empirical confirmation is central to perceived strength.",
    "R1.3": "Claims such as maintaining accuracy and achieving 30–40% parameter reduction should be precisely aligned with the reported numbers and ablation.",
    "R1.4": "A stronger positioning requires clarifying the delta versus GraphFlow‑v1 and nearby typed-edge GNNs to avoid ambiguous novelty.",
    "R1.6": "Concise, high‑impact wording and improved flow typically raise the perceived strength without altering content.",
    "RX.1": "We should explicitly acknowledge uncertainties (e.g., small accuracy deltas) to avoid unwarranted confidence.",
    "RX.2": "Avoiding overstatement is critical given modest improvements and limited benchmarks.",
    "RX.3": "All work should stay within the scope of ‘make this stronger’ without introducing new results or claims.",
    "RX.4": "Preventing hype that could mislead practitioners about deployability mitigates downstream harm.",
    "RX.5": "Any strengthened statements must remain traceable to the artifact’s reported sections, numbers, and ablation."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To make this stronger, should the focus be (A) wording-only polish for clarity and punch without changing structure or claims, (B) reframing the contribution/novelty and tightening the argument (may rephrase strength of claims), or (C) sharpening evidence–claim alignment and quantitative emphasis (calibrating claims to the reported deltas and parameter counts)? You can choose one or multiple (A/B/C).

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening likely involves sharpening the thesis and contribution framing so the core innovation and its motivation are unmistakable.
rationale.R1.2: Ensuring a tight argument from the parameter-sharing idea to expected benefits and empirical confirmation is central to perceived strength.
rationale.R1.3: Claims such as maintaining accuracy and achieving 30–40% parameter reduction should be precisely aligned with the reported numbers and ablation.
rationale.R1.4: A stronger positioning requires clarifying the delta versus GraphFlow‑v1 and nearby typed-edge GNNs to avoid ambiguous novelty.
rationale.R1.6: Concise, high‑impact wording and improved flow typically raise the perceived strength without altering content.
rationale.RX.1: We should explicitly acknowledge uncertainties (e.g., small accuracy deltas) to avoid unwarranted confidence.
rationale.RX.2: Avoiding overstatement is critical given modest improvements and limited benchmarks.
rationale.RX.3: All work should stay within the scope of ‘make this stronger’ without introducing new results or claims.
rationale.RX.4: Preventing hype that could mislead practitioners about deployability mitigates downstream harm.
rationale.RX.5: Any strengthened statements must remain traceable to the artifact’s reported sections, numbers, and ablation.
